preFix.title: Needle-felted barn owl
preFix.subtitle: A perching owl in white, buff, and warm grey
preFix.excerpt: A 15 cm perching barn owl with a distinctive white heart-shaped face, buff and grey upperparts, and sparsely spotted white underparts. Wire leg armature holds the perching pose on a small twig. Layered needle felting with different wool tops achieves colour accuracy.
preFix.sourceNotes: Synthesised from UK guild needle-felted bird construction methods. Wire leg armature for perching poses and heart-face construction are standard intermediate owl-pattern techniques. Primary references: guild teaching notes and published needle-felting pattern guides; no single public domain text.

content.text: The barn owl's heart-shaped facial disc is one of the most recognisable shapes in British wildlife, and it translates beautifully into needle felting. At 15 cm tall this is a manageable intermediate project: large enough to show detail, small enough to complete in a focused weekend. The
armature
here is limited to the legs and feet; the body itself is solid felted form. You will work through the body core, surface layering in buff and grey
wool tops
, the white face disc, and finally the facial detail.

attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. Enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.
attrs.title: Needle safety

attrs.body: Bend all cut wire ends back with pliers before wrapping in roving. Exposed wire ends can pierce the finished surface.
attrs.title: Wire handling

content.text: You will need: 60 g white
merino
roving
for the body core;
wool tops
in warm buff, pale grey, golden-brown, white, and black; 0.5 mm galvanised wire for the leg
armature
; two 6 mm amber or yellow plastic safety eyes; craft pliers; and a small twig or dowel for mounting. The twig should be 10-15 cm long and 1-2 cm in diameter.
Method

Body core. Roll 45 g of white merino roving into a firm upright oval approximately 10 cm tall and 5 cm wide. This is the body of the owl; barn owls are plump and round-chested. Place on your foam pad and stab all over with a 38-gauge needle, rotating the form constantly. Work until the oval holds its shape without springing back when squeezed.

Head. Roll a ball of white merino approximately 4 cm across. Felt separately until firm. The barn owl's head is almost as wide as the body when the face disc is included. Hold the head against the top of the body: it should sit forward slightly, giving the characteristic forward-facing posture. Felt the join from all angles until solid.
Upperpart colour. Lay thin wisps of warm buff
wool tops

over the back, top of the head, and wings. Work in thin layers, building up colour gradually. Over the buff base, add thin wisps of pale grey on the wings and back; barn owls show a complex soft grey-and-buff pattern on the upperparts rather than a single uniform colour. Use a 38-gauge needle for the initial layering and a 42-gauge for blending the colour boundary.
White underparts. Leave the chest, belly, and underside of the wings in white. Add a few very small, sparse dark spots on the chest using black
wool tops
worked in with a 42-gauge needle. Barn owls have very few spots on the underparts; less is more here.

Heart-shaped face disc. The face disc is the defining feature. Using white wool tops, build up a raised oval area on the front of the head, approximately 3 cm wide and 3.5 cm tall. Felt in firmly so it sits slightly proud of the surrounding surface. Then, using a 42-gauge needle and a thin wisp of buff or golden-brown wool tops, outline the disc: start at the top centre of the head, work down the left side in a curved line to the chin, then up the right side, completing the heart shape. The outline should be about 3-4 mm wide and clearly defined.

Eyes and beak. Push the safety eye posts through the face disc, one on each side of the centre line, at approximately one-third of the way down from the top of the disc. The eyes should be level and symmetrical. Press the washers on firmly from behind using a blunt darning needle. The beak is small and heart-shaped in barn owls: felt a tiny triangle of pale golden-brown or cream wool tops just below the centre point between the eyes, using a 42-gauge needle.

Leg armature. Cut two 10 cm lengths of 0.5 mm wire. Bend each into a foot shape: three forward toes and one backward toe (hallux), each about 1.5 cm long. Bend the cut ends back with pliers. Wrap each leg and foot tightly in buff or pale grey roving. Felt the wrapped wire by pressing it into the foam pad and stabbing along each toe until the wrapping is firm. Push the leg wires up into the base of the body and felt in firmly, building up a small mound of roving around each leg base to cover the entry point.

Mounting. Bend the feet around your twig so the toes grip naturally. A barn owl grips with two toes forward and two back (zygodactyl grip), but for display purposes the three-forward-one-back arrangement is acceptable and looks convincing. Add a small amount of matching roving around the grip point and felt in to secure the feet to the branch.

items.fix: Add more white wool tops in thin layers, felting each layer in from the centre outward. The disc should be visibly higher than the surrounding buff surface.
items.cause: Not enough wool tops were built up in the disc area
items.symptom: Face disc looks flat rather than raised

items.fix: Pull the boundary wisp as fine as possible before placing it. Use a 42-gauge needle and work in short strokes along the line rather than trying to felt it all at once.
items.cause: The boundary wisp was too thick to control
items.symptom: Heart-shaped outline looks wobbly or uneven

items.fix: Mark the eye positions lightly with a pin before committing. Once safety eye washers are on, the eyes cannot be moved without tearing the surface.
items.cause: The eye positions were not measured before pushing the posts through
items.symptom: Eyes are not level with each other

items.fix: Bend the outer toes further around the branch with pliers after positioning, then felt additional roving over the grip point to lock the feet in place.
items.cause: The wire toes do not grip tightly enough around the twig diameter
items.symptom: Owl will not stay on the branch

content.text: Display and care
The mounted owl on its twig can be displayed on a shelf or hung on a wall. The
barbs

on felting needles dull faster on dense surfaces; replace needles as soon as they start dragging. The white areas of the finished piece will yellow slightly over many years in sunlight; display out of direct light to slow this process. Keep away from moths: wool felts are susceptible, particularly undyed and white fleece.

initialFindings.message: tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
paragraph reads at grade 11.2 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
